heading: CXLVI NOTE AL SAGGIO INTRODUTTIVO
card_summary: This chunk consists of Saverio Campanini's scholarly endnotes (notes 279–303) to his introductory essay on Zorzi's De Harmonia Mundi. The notes document the Trinitarian interpretation of the Tetragrammaton (YHWH) as a three-letter name whose repetition of He encodes the unity of three divine persons, tracing this argument through Raymond Martini's Pugio Fidei, Petrus Alphonsus, Porchetus de Salvaticus's Victoria adversus Hebraeos, and Pietro Galatino's De arcanis catholicae veritatis. Further notes treat the concept of "circular numbers" (5, 6, 10) as understood by Cassiodorus, Isidore of Seville, and Reuchlin's De arte cabalistica, as well as kabbalistic associations of the sefirot with planets, with body parts of the cosmic anthropos (Adam Kadmon), and with the divine middot/attributes. Campanini also notes the reception of Zorzi's Tetragrammaton topos by the Spanish Jesuit Blasius Viegas, and flags an interesting polemic by French Christian kabbalist Jacques Gaffarel against the axiom that one can only give what one has.
essay:
## Overview

This chunk comprises Campanini's endnotes 279–303 to his introductory essay on Francesco Zorzi's *De Harmonia Mundi*. Rather than Zorzi's own text, these are the editor's dense scholarly annotations, documenting sources, parallels, and reception history for the argument that the Tetragrammaton encodes the Christian Trinity, and for several related kabbalistic and arithmological themes. The notes run across pages 146–149 of the Bompiani 2010 edition and constitute a miniature research dossier.

## The Tetragrammaton and the Trinity (Notes 279–285)

The central cluster of notes (279–284) documents a tradition of Trinitarian exegesis of the divine four-letter name (YHWH — *yod, he, vav, he*), arguing that the name is in truth composed of only three distinct letters, with He repeated, and that this structure encodes the Trinity of persons in unity of essence.

**Note 279** cites a text from the *Patrologia Latina* (vol. CCVII, col. 833A–333B), which Campanini cross-references with Williams (1935). The quoted Latin runs:

> Praeterea illud nomen Dei, quod in secretis secretorum apud Judaeos ineffabile est, et tetragrammaton dicitur, licet quatuor figurarum, trium tantummodo elementorum Io, he, vaf, he namque bis ponitur. Si ergo diligenter hoc nomen inspicias, trinum est in Hebraeo et unum.

<cite>p. 146, note 279</cite>

The passage continues to show how each pairing of adjacent letters (Yod-He, He-Vav, Vav-He) yields a distinct name of God, yet all four letters together yield only one name — a structure read as the Trinity of persons in one substance (*unitatem in tribus personis ejusdem substantiae*).

**Note 280** draws on Raymond Martini's *Pugio Fidei* (Leipzig 1687 edition, *Tertia pars*, dist. III, cap. IV, p. 685), which Campanini notes was widely circulated in manuscript during the Renaissance. Martini's argument — relying on the convert **Petrus Alphonsus**, a Spanish rabbi who became Christian — is that the three primary letters of the Tetragrammaton signify three divine proprietates (*middot*), while the repeated He, identified with the Hebrew word *havayah* (essence/being), signifies the unity of essence underlying those three properties:

> Una vero earum quae repetitur, et in fine nominis ponitur, quae est he, et est prima in hoc nomine Essentia, indicat trium midot id est, proprietatum vel personarum unitatem Essentiae.

<cite>p. 146–147, note 280</cite>

**Note 281** presents a parallel passage from **Porchetus de Salvaticus** (*Victoria Porcheti adversus impios Hebraeos*, Paris 1520, pars II, cap. VIII, p. 71r), which similarly credits Petrus Alphonsus and rehearses the same argument: the three prior letters (*Yod, He, Vav*) indicate three mutually distinct divine properties, while the repeated He (*havayah* = essence) indicates their unity.

**Note 284** moves to **Pietro Galatino** (*De arcanis catholicae veritatis*, Ortona a Mare 1518, Lib. II, cap. x), who provides the most elaborate version of the argument. Each letter is given a specific Trinitarian identification: *Yod* (meaning "principle," as the origin of all written letters, taking its origin from none) signifies the Father; *He* (meaning "to be" or "to live") signifies the Son, through whom all things came into being; *Vav* (meaning the copulative conjunction "and") signifies the Holy Spirit as the bond of love between Father and Son:

> Tertia autem scilicet vau, quae copulativam dictionem Et denotat, spiritum sanctum optime exprimit, qui cum sit amor patris et filii, quo se vicissim amant, recte copula, id est nexus utriusque nuncupatur.

<cite>p. 148, note 284</cite>

**Note 285** observes that Joseph de Voisin, in the margin of his commented edition of the *Pugio Fidei* (Leipzig 1687), already noted that Galatino derived his argument directly from Martini: *Hinc desumpsit Galatinus*.

## Cross-References within De Harmonia Mundi (Notes 286–287, 296, 301–303)

Several notes (286, 287, 296, 301–303) simply provide cross-references to other passages in *De Harmonia Mundi* itself, indicating that the Tetragrammaton interpretation, the association of the sefirot with planets (1,4,2), the body-parts of the cosmic anthropos (1,6,4), and the Divine Word as "sheath" (*vagina*) of the holy name (*tal*, dew, as sheath of the Tetragrammaton, 1,7,18) are recurring structural nodes in Zorzi's larger cosmological project. Note 286 gives: *De harmonia mundi*, Proemio, 1,1,10; 1,6,34; 1,7,21; 1,7,29.

## Circular Numbers: Arithmetic and Kabbalah (Notes 290–293)

Notes 290–293 annotate Zorzi's use of "circular numbers" — numbers that, when multiplied by themselves, return to their own final digit. The concept is defined by Cassiodorus (*Institutiones* 2,4) and Isidore of Seville (*Etymologiae* 3,7,1):

> Circularis numerus est ita, qui dum similiter multiplicatus fuerit, a se inchoans ad se convertitur, ut verbi gratia quinquies quini xxv.

<cite>p. 148, note 291</cite>

The circular numbers identified are 5, 6, and 10. Note 292 connects this to **Johann Reuchlin**'s *De arte cabalistica* (Hagenau 1517), which uses the Pythagorean figure of a circle of ten numerical figures centered on five (the midpoint of the decade) to show that every pair of numbers equidistant from five sums to ten — a kabbalistic-arithmological reading of the *sefirot* as a self-enclosed circular system.

A manuscript source (Piancastelli VII 57, Biblioteca Comunale di Forlì, f. 61v) is cited in note 293: *tres sunt numeri spherici idest qui replicati revertuntur et concludunt idem. Primus est quinarius, secundus senarius, tertius denarius.*

Note 294 returns to Reuchlin's *De arte cabalistica* for the identification of *Yod* (the tenth letter, whose numerical value is 10) with the tenth *sefirah* (*Issim*, "nobles and patricians"), linking alphabetic, numerical, and sefirotic symbolism.

## Reception History and Polemical Aside (Notes 292, 295, 298)

Note 295 traces the reception of Zorzi's Tetragrammaton topos forward in time to the Spanish Jesuit **Blasius Viegas** (*Commentarii exegetici in Apocalypsin*, Évora 1601; referenced via François Secret 1985, p. 224), showing that Zorzi's Christianizing kabbalism remained influential into the seventeenth century.

Note 298 flags an intellectually notable polemic: the French Christian kabbalist **Jacques Gaffarel** (1601–1681), in his *Nihil, fere nihil, minus nihilo* (Venice 1634), argued against the apparently obvious axiom that one can only give what one possesses, claiming instead that what is transmitted from father to son is precisely the universal character, not the individual *proprium* — a curious philosophical inversion with kabbalistic implications for the transmission of divine attributes. Campanini cross-references his own study (Campanini 2007c).

## Kabbalistic Sources for the Sefirot-Middot Equation (Notes 299–300)

Note 299 identifies the concept of divine *middot* (attributes = *sefirot*) as found already in **Isaac the Blind** (*Yitzhaq Sagi Nahor*), and in Mosheh Botarel's commentary on the *Sefer Yetzirah* (Warsaw 1884). Note 300 mentions the *Sefer ha-Peli'ah* (also referenced in note 297, ed. Jerusalem 1997, p. 93) in connection with Zorzi's catalogue of kabbalistic books, noting that in the Italian translation of *De Harmonia Mundi*, *middot* is rendered as "attributi."

## Significance for Zorzi's Project

These notes collectively reveal the dense intertextual scaffolding behind Zorzi's Trinitarian-kabbalistic argument. The Tetragrammaton exegesis — tracing through Petrus Alphonsus, Martini, Porchetus, and Galatino — represents a sustained Christian kabbalist tradition that Zorzi inherits and systematizes. The "circular number" motif connects Neopythagorean arithmetic (Cassiodorus, Isidore) with Reuchlinian kabbalism and with Zorzi's broader harmonic cosmology, in which numerical self-enclosure mirrors divine self-sufficiency. The cross-references to De Harmonia Mundi 1,4,2 (sefirot-planets) and 1,6,4 (sefirot-body-parts of the cosmic anthropos) confirm that the Tetragrammaton's inner structure is not an isolated proof-text but a generating matrix for Zorzi's entire harmonic system.
